content:
Values of a set of data (random samples) are normally distributed. Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

(i) Most data values are clustered near the mean, giving the distribution a well-defined single peak.
(ii) The value of the mean and the value of the median are very close or even identical. a well-defined single peak.
(iii) Calculation of standard deviation of a data set provides a good measurement of the dispersion of the data.
(iv) Larger deviations from the mean become increasing rare, producing the tapering tails of the distribution.
All of the statements (i), (ii), (iii), and (iv) are true for a normally distributed set of data.

content: Cholesterol levels in men from 18 to 24 years of age are normally distributed with a mean of 178 and a standard deviation of 41. John is 20 years old. His cholesterol level is 273. What percentage (correct to one decimal place) of men have a cholesterol level > 273?

We can begin by finding the Z-score for John's cholesterol level:

Z = (X - μ) / σ = (273 - 178) / 41 = 2.32 (approx.)

Using a Z-table or a calculator, we can find that the area to the right of Z = 2.32 is 0.0107. This means that the percentage of men with a cholesterol level above 273 is:

P(Z > 2.32) = 0.0107 = 1.1% (approx.)

Therefore, approximately 1.1% of men in this age range have a cholesterol level above 273.

content:
Which of the following statement(s) about observational studies is/are false?

(i) Researchers observe or measure characteristics of the sample members but do not attempt to influence or modify these characteristics.
(ii) Observational studies are particularly useful when it is not ethical to carry out experimental studies.
(iii) In observational studies, members of a sample can be divided into a treatment group and a control group. Researchers give a treatment to each members of the treatment group.
(iv) Survey is one type of observation studies.

The statement (iii) is false. In observational studies, there is no control over which group a person is assigned to, so researchers cannot create a treatment group and a control group. Rather, they simply observe and measure characteristics of the sample members.

content:
In statistical studies, which of following statements are describing “confounding factor” correctly?

(i) “Confounding factor” is a factor which is distributed non-randomly with respect to the independent (exposure) or dependent (outcome) variable which is the subject of an enquiry.
(ii) “Confound factor” is an extraneous factor in a research study that correlates with both the dependent and independent variables.
(iii) Effects of confounding factors on the statistics can be minimized by randomly drawing subjects from the population to form the study samples.
(iv) When compared to experimental studies, observation studies are more susceptible to the effects of confounding factors.

The statements (i) and (ii) are both correct in describing "confounding factor" in statistical studies. Statement (i) correctly describes that the confounding factor is related to the independent or dependent variable being studied and is not distributed randomly among the groups being compared. Statement (ii) correctly describes that the confounding factor is a factor that is extraneous to the research question, but that affects both the dependent and independent variable, making it difficult to determine causality. 

Statement (iii) is false. Randomly drawing subjects from the population to form the study sample cannot control for confounding factors that are related to the variables being studied. Instead, researchers need to carefully design their studies and take measurements of potential confounding factors in order to control for these effects.

Statement (iv) is generally true. In observational studies, confounding factors are more likely to influence the results because researchers cannot control which individuals are assigned to the treatment and control groups. In experimental studies, researchers can randomly assign individuals to groups in order to control for potential confounding factors.

moves.text_span: The statements (i) and (ii) are both correct in describing "confounding factor"...

content:
When comparing the body weights of a group of boys and a group of girls, a statistical test is used to produce a P-value. Assuming the null hypothesis is that the two study groups are the same. What does the P-value indicate?

A.
The probability of “the alternate hypothesis is true”.

B.
The probability of “the null hypothesis is true”.

C.
The probability of “the observed body weights assuming the two study groups are not different”.

D.
The probability of “the observed body weights assuming the two study groups are different”.

C. The P-value indicates the probability of observing the data (or data more extreme) assuming that the null hypothesis is true (i.e. assuming that the two study groups are not different in terms of body weight). It does not directly provide information about the probability of the null or alternate hypothesis being true.

content:
Which of the following statement(s) about “correlation” is/are false?

(i) The more closely two variables follow the same increasing trend, the stronger the correlation.
(ii) In a positive correlation, two variables tend to increase (or decrease) in the same direction.
(iii) In a negative correlation, two variables tend to increase (or decrease) in the opposite directions.
(iv) A positive correlation (statistically significant, P-value < 0.05) indicates a “cause and effect” relationship.

A.
(i) and (iii)

B.
(i) only

C.
(iv) only

D.
None of them

moves.text_span:

(i) only

C.
(iv) only

D.
None of them

content: C. Statement (iv) is false. A positive correlation between two variables indicates that they tend to increase or decrease together, but it does not necessarily mean that one variable causes the other. A correlation does not imply a causal relationship, and it is important to establish causality through other research methods.

content:
Car A costs 30% more than Car B. Car C costs 25% less than Car A. Which car is cheapest?

A.
Cannot be determined from the information given

B.
Car B

C.
Car C

D.
Car A

moves.text_span:
ar is cheapest?

A.
Cannot be determined from the information give

content:
To determine which car is cheapest, we need to compare their prices. Let the price of Car B be x.

Car A costs 30% more than Car B, so its price is:

Price of Car A = x + 0.3x = 1.3x

Car C costs 25% less than Car A, so its price is:

Price of Car C = 0.75 × (Price of Car A) = 0.75 × 1.3x = 0.975x

To compare prices, we can simplify the expressions:

- Price of Car B = x
- Price of Car A = 1.3x
- Price of Car C = 0.975x

Since x > 0, we can rank the prices as:

Price of Car B < Price of Car C < Price of Car A

Therefore, Car C is the cheapest option. The answer is (C).

content:
In mathematical modeling, which of the following statement(s) about “function” is/are false?

(i) Independent variables are variables that are input into a function.
(ii) Values of independent variables are not controlled by a function.
(iii) Dependent variable is a variable that is output from a function.
(iv) Value of a dependent variable is controlled by the independent variable(s) and the function

None of the statements are false. All the statements describe correctly the concept of a function in mathematical modeling. 

(i) Independent variables are the input variables of a function.
(ii) Values of independent variables are typically not controlled by the function, but rather are the input values chosen by the modeler or obtained in a specific case.
(iii) Dependent variable is the output of a function, a variable that depends on the value of the independent variable(s).
(iv) The value of the dependent variable is controlled by the independent variable(s) and the function which relates the values of these variables. The dependent variable changes as the independent variables change according to the function.

content:
Anson wants to build a mathematical model to reflect the happiness of the elderly. He believes that health and wealth are the key factors affecting the happiness of the elderly. Therefore, he plans to measure the health index, amount of bank savings, and happy index of a group of old people. Concerning Anson’s model, which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

(i) Happy index is the independent variable.
(ii) Health index and amount of bank savings are the dependent variables.
(iii) Anson’s model can be presented as “Happy Index is a function of health index and amount of bank savings”.br> (iv) Anson’s model can be presented mathematically as “Happy Index = f(health index, amount of bank savings).

The correct statements are (iii) and (iv).

(i) Happy index cannot be the independent variable because it is what is being predicted or explained by the model.

(ii) Health index and amount of bank savings cannot be the dependent variables because they are the predictors or explanatory variables.

(iii) Anson's model is correctly stated as "Happy index is a function of health index and amount of bank savings". This means that both health index and amount of bank savings together influence the happiness index of the elderly.

(iv) Anson's model is best represented mathematically as "Happy index = f(health index, amount of bank savings)", which shows that the happiness index is a function (f) of the health index and amount of bank savings.

moves.text_span:
The correct statements are (iii) and (iv).

content:
Which of the following graphs is the best way for presenting/summarizing the key information of a set of data which (1) are in the continuous scale, and (2) are positively skewed?

A.
Spider chart

B.
Bar chart with error bars

C.
Pie chart

D.
Boxplot

D. Boxplot is the best way for presenting/summarizing the key information of a set of data which (1) are in the continuous scale, and (2) are positively skewed. A boxplot provides a clear visual summary of the distribution of the data, including the median, quartiles, and any outliers. It also highlights the skewness of the data through the length and position of the whiskers. A spider chart is more suited for displaying multiple variables, a Bar chart with error bars and Pie chart may not provide an adequate visualization of the distribution of continuous, positively skewed data.

content: The Consumer Price Index (2018/2019-based index) of Oct 2020 was 105, and the Consumer Price Index (2018/2019-based index) of Oct 2021 was 116. The price of a dozen of apples was MOP70.0 in Oct 2021. Estimate the price of a dozen apples in Oct 2020 (rounding off to the nearest 10 cents).

We can use the inflation formula to estimate the price of a dozen apples in Oct 2020 based on the given information:

New Price = Old Price × (New CPI / Old CPI)

We want to find the Old Price, so we can rearrange the formula:

Old Price = New Price × (Old CPI / New CPI)

Substituting the given values,

Old Price = MOP70.0 × (105 / 116) ≈ MOP63.64

Rounding to the nearest 10 cents, the estimated price of a dozen apples in Oct 2020 is MOP63.60.

content:
You have MOP 100,000 to invest into the stock market. You want to spend all the money to buy the stock from one of the four companies, which are Company A, Company B, Company C and Company D. The following table summarizes the predicted annual profits per MOP 100,000 of stock purchase for the stocks of the four companies. You want to invest using the Minimax Regret Strategy. Which company’s stock should you purchase?

Company A Stock
Bull Market: $7,500
Bear Market:-$2,000
Company B Stock
Bull Market:$5,000
Bear Market:$0
Company C Stock
Bull Market:$4,000
Bear Market:$1,000
Company D Stock
Bull Market:$2,000
Bear Market:$1,500

moves.text_span: rket. You want to spend all the money to buy the stock from one of the four c
[shift to: To determine which company's stock to purchase using the Minimax Regret Strategy]
e stock market. You want to spend all the money to buy the stock fr

content:
To use the Minimax Regret Strategy, we first need to calculate the regret values for each choice. The regret is the opportunity cost of a decision, which is calculated as the difference between the maximum payoff and the payoff of a particular decision. In this case, the maximum payoff is the maximum profit achieved among all four companies, regardless of whether the market is a bull market or a bear market.

For example, the regret of choosing Company A in a bull market is:

Regret of Company A in a bull market = Maximum payoff - Payoff of Company A in a bull market

= $7,500 - $7,500 = $0

The regret of choosing Company A in a bear market is:

Regret of Company A in a bear market = Maximum payoff - Payoff of Company A in a bear market

= $7,500 - (-$2,000) = $9,500

Similarly, we can calculate the regrets for all other companies and market conditions:

Regret of Company B in a bull market = $3,500
Regret of Company B in a bear market = $5,000
Regret of Company C in a bull market = $3,500
Regret of Company C in a bear market = $4,000
Regret of Company D in a bull market = $5,500
Regret of Company D in a bear market = $4,000

Next, we need to calculate the maximum regret for each market condition. For a bull market, the maximum regret is $5,500, which is the regret of choosing Company D. For a bear market, the maximum regret is $5,000, which is the regret of choosing Company B.

Finally, we choose the company that minimizes the maximum regret. In this case, Company C has the smallest maximum regret of $3,500, which occurs in both bull and bear markets. Therefore, you should purchase the stock of Company C.

content:
In statistical studies, which of following statements are describing “confounding factor” correctly?

(i) “Confounding factor” is a factor which is distributed non-randomly with respect to the independent (exposure) or dependent (outcome) variable which is the subject of an enquiry.
(ii) “Confound factor” is an extraneous factor in a research study that correlates with both the dependent and independent variables.
(iii) Effects of confounding factors on the statistics can be minimized by randomly drawing subjects from the population to form the study samples.
(iv) When compared to experimental studies, observation studies are more susceptible to the effects of confounding factors.

The statements (i) and (ii) are both correct in describing "confounding factor" in statistical studies. Statement (i) correctly describes that the confounding factor is related to the independent or dependent variable being studied and is not distributed randomly among the groups being compared. Statement (ii) correctly describes that the confounding factor is a factor that is extraneous to the research question, but that affects both the dependent and independent variable, making it difficult to determine causality. 

Statement (iii) is false. Randomly drawing subjects from the population to form the study sample cannot control for confounding factors that are related to the variables being studied. Instead, researchers need to carefully design their studies and take measurements of potential confounding factors in order to control for these effects.

Statement (iv) is generally true. In observational studies, confounding factors are more likely to influence the results because researchers cannot control which individuals are assigned to the treatment and control groups. In experimental studies, researchers can randomly assign individuals to groups in order to control for potential confounding factors.
